             The ornaments found in the urnfields vary so greatly from
          group to group that a detailed description of them must be
 Fibulae postponed. Here it suffices to say that fibulae are only found
     rare sporadically, chiefly in the Austro-Bavarian group, and to
    Gold draw attention to the comparative abundance of gold ob-
          jects. Farther west round Plzen, in Bavaria, and the Tyrol
          the precious metal is represented principally by small helical
          wire rings generally of type 1a. But a gold disk ornamented
          with stamped concentric circles was found in a Tyrolese
          urnfield, and the grave at Binningen (Canton Basel) and the
          hoard of Paseka' in Western Bohemia contained large oval
        sheets of gold leaf, likewise ornamented with stamped circles,
         here supplemented by tangents, zigzags, and triangles in relief
         (Fig. 193). Perhaps these were originally intended as platings
         on knife sheathes.?
  Amber     Amber} and glass beads are also sometimes found.
 Bronze     Further evidence of the wealth of the urnfield folk is afforded
   vases by the bronze vases. Smooth shallow cups with ribbon handles
         riveted on were found in the Tyrolese cemeteries, at Griin-
         wald, and in Western Bohemia, all in the earliest phase. The
         most striking vessel of the early group, however, is the famous
Wheeled wheeled bowl from Milavet.5 It approximates in form to the
   bowls usual cinerary urn, but stands on a framework to which four
         wheels (four-spoked) are attached. Parallels are known from
         North Germany and Denmark, but the whole group has very
         plausibly been connected with Hittite and Phoenician cult
         objects.°
            Though both types of bronze vessel are usually designated
         Italian, no really exact parallels are known in the Peninsula.
         Even the large buckets, ornamented with birds’ protomae, that
         appear just a little later,’ have more numerous parallels in
         North-Eastern Hungary than in Italy, though the ornament was
         very popular in the latter country in Benacci I times. And in
         Bavaria such buckets are associated with gold vases far more
         likely to have come from Hungary than Italy.

   Settlements of the early urnfield folk are less well known. Settle-
The sites (e.g. round Innsbruck) seem generally to have been ments
chosen with a view to defence. If fortification is not demon-
strated with certainty for the earliest phase, there is no doubt
that the early South German ‘ring walls’ of the next period
were built by the urnfield folk.

Federsee, and Hallwil in Switzerland (Aargau).3 On the other

described in detail, but their interpretation as refuse pits or
silos still seems possible.
   The mines and industrial settlements deserve a section to Hoards
themselves. The hoards connected with their exploitation have
the same character as the contemporary depots described in
Chapter XVI. They seem to have been left by travelling
tinkers, engaged in the collection of old scrap bronze for re-
smelting, and include many worn implements as well as form-
less lumps of bronze.
                   Il. THE     COPPER     AND   SALT       MINES

We have connected the prehistoric copper mines of the Tyrol
and Salzburg and the salt mines of the Salzkammergut with
the urnfield people. Their cemeteries spread in a significant
manner all along the Grauwack zone of the Eastern Alps,5 and
 « AuhV. v, p- 396, fig. 2, h; Schu-     Eisenbichl near Reichenhall, MAGW
 macher, Rheinlande, p. 69.              xxxiv, p. 60.
 2 Kraft, BY. 131, p. 189.               4 Verh. OP. \xviii, p. 37-
 3 Schumacher, Rheinlande, i, p. 69;     ° BRGK. xvi, p. 22.
 Reinerth, Wohnbau;      perhaps also
             340             THE       NORTH    ALPINE   URNFIELDS
           the exploitation of the mines in the urnfield period is admitted
           by all authorities.                                 OF oe
      The     Mining? was     carried out by  means   of open workings  and
    mines also by regular shafts and underground galleries when the
           surface lodes had been exhausted. The shafts were generally
           slanting, and sometimes a second or even a third shaft was cut
           toimprovethedraught. Where the softness of the stone required
           it, the walls of the shafts and galleries were strengthened with
           props. Generally it was found sufficient to fix stout piles
           spanning the gap between floor and roof or between the
           walls by means of a wedge, but in very soft stone the walls of
           the galleries were supported by planks behind the props.’
 Methods      In driving the shafts the rock was first split by kindling a
        of fire  against it and then throwing water on the hot surface.
   mining The miner’s chief implement was a sort of short bronze crow-
             bar socketed to take a wooden shaft. Many knee-axe shafts,
             designed to fit bronze celts with the wings at, or generally
             above, the middle of the body, have been found at Hallstatt
             and at Diirnberg near Hallein. These axes would have been
             used for cutting the timbers.
 Grooved        In the copper mines the ore was roughly broken up and
 hammer-     washed before transportation to the surface. Grooved hammer-
    stones   stones (cf. Fig. 130) were used for breaking up the ore, and the
             refined product was then carried to the surface in wooden
             troughs or leather sacks. The ascent was facilitated by a
             ladder made from a tree-trunk with notches cut in it to serve
             as steps.
 Smelting       The copper ore was smelted close to the shaft, but the ovens
             were always near streamlets. The furnaces were apparently
             built into the side of the mountain, and probably stood about
             a metre high. There was a depression on the floor of the oven
             where the molten metal might accumulate. It is questionable
             whether any special channel was provided to allow the slag to
             run off; perhaps the oven wall was opened up periodically,
             and the slag raked out. The Mitterberg ore contains such a
             high sulphur percentage that it easily burns by itself. All
             that would be needed would be to light a fire on the oven’s
           hearth. and then fill up the furnace with alternate layers of ore
           and charcoal.
Age of the _ As noted in Chapter VII, Much believed that the exploita-
    mines tion of the Tyrolese copper ores began
                                                    in our Period III. The
             This account is based      on Andree,   2 Andree, p. 34.
             Bergbau in der Vorzeit.                 3 Klose in OKT. xvii, p. 32.
                THE NORTH       ALPINE   URNFIELDS               341
Late Bronze Age mining industry just described would, on
this view, be the ultimate development of the old surface
workings. Kyrle, as we saw, was inclined to reject the Copper
Age exploitation altogether. As to the regular mining, his
conclusions are very definite. The copper mines were all
in operation simultaneously; the objects found in them are
everywhere the same; the methods of working show no
trace of development. ‘The technique of mining’, he con-
cludes, ‘was not autochthonous; it was introduced by experi-
enced miners after the discovery of the ores.’* It would be
natural to identify the initiators of the new industry with the
urnfield folk.
    The remains from the mines do not settle the question, Bronzes
but if the bronzes found stray or in settlements in the mining from
region be examined they certainly support the view just quoted. the
Apart from a couple of hoards, obviously connected with the region
amber trade across the Brenner, Early Bronze Age types are
conspicuously lacking. On the other hand, Late Bronze Age
types (swords, vase-head pins, ribbed and twisted bracelets),
characteristic of the urnfield culture, are very common.
    They do not, however, clinch the issue. Hell of Salzburg Pottery
has recently revived—in a modified form—Much’s theories in
a series of important articles based upon a study of the pottery
from settlements some of which indubitably are connected
with the mining industry. The settlements are for the most
part hill stations, and all yielded chalcolithic remains of the
Altheim culture.
    From the Rainberg near Salzburg,’ perhaps also from the Middle
 Klinglberg3 (near the Mitterberg mines), and the Grillberg Bronze
                                                                 Age
 (near Elsbethen),* come sherds resembling the pottery of the sherds
Middle Bronze Age barrows in Bavaria and Oberpfalz. Simi-
 lar wares occur at Reichenhall just across the Bavarian border
 accompanied by bronzes characteristic of the older Bavarian
 tumuli.S Finally, some sherds from Gétschenberg near Bischof-
 shofen’ and elsewhere—especially fragments of jugs with a
 shoulder-groove as in the Eastern Aunjetitz group—might Aunetitz
 really be Early Bronze Age. Metal forms belonging to that forms
 period are represented bya flanged celt of type At from Hell-
 brunner hill near Salzburg,7 and a pin of type B2 from

          Rainberg.t Hence Hell concludes that the arrival of skilled
          miners in the Austrian Alps should be dated back to our
          Period IV.
   Late     The bulk of the remains from the settlements would, how-
 Bronze   ever, seem to belong to the. Late Bronze Age. Among the
     Age abundant coarse sherds appear fragments of an urn-like pithos
    types with concave neck.2 The neck is generally smooth, the body
dominant roughened and separated from the neck byaplastic ridge set
          off with finger-tip impression. This form of pithos occurs    in
          the Late Bronze Age barrow of Riegsee 23 in Upper Bavaria 3
          and in the settlements round Reichenhall+ (Fig. 192 a), and
          then in many contemporary settlements in Lower Austria5
          and Bohemia.® It is no doubt related to pithoi of the Aunje-
          titz period,’ but is equally akin to the ossuary from our urn-
          fields. A wider form, associated with the foregoing at Eisen-
          bichl near Reichenhall (Fig. 192 b), is actually found in early
          settlements of that type.? The definite association of this type
          of vase with the mining settlements gives therefore no con-
          clusive evidence as to the origin of the miners. They might
          have brought it with them from Hungary in the Early Bronze
          Age or from somewhere else in the Late Bronze Age. The
          fact remains that the urnfield folk did eventually settle round
          the mines. Prior to their advent there is no evidence for inten-
          sive settlement, still less for any wealth of local or imported
          bronzes in the Eastern Alps. If the ores had been early ex-
          ploited we should reasonably expect to find in such a well-
          studied area a mass of imported luxury articles of older type
          than actually do meet our eyes in the urnfield period. The
          evidence therefore favours Kyrle’s view.
                               III.    THE    SEPARATE     GROUPS
          (a) The Austro-Bavarian Group.
          The urnfields of the Upper Danube basin from Lower Austria
          to Switzerland constitute a very consistent group, distinguished
          by the bronzes and accessory vases from the Bohemian and
          Franconian series.                           .

  The accessory vases comprise a considerable variety of Accessory
types.    Dishes were not often used to cover the urns and so vases:
 are not prominent. The commonest form has a collar and forms
 broad rim.’ Cups appear in three varieties: (2) a shallow
 form in which the handle falls from the rim to the shoulder
 as in Lausitz* (Fig. 194a), (3) a variant in which the handle
 begins mid-way up the neck; more rarely (4) the handle sits
 on the shoulder. Small pots (5), not unlike the urn in shape,
 appear in several urnfields, especially in Baden and Switzer-
 land.3 The jug (6) (Fig. 196) resembles the cup (2), save that
 the neck is taller. Among interesting variants may be noted
 small urns of Villanova shape (Fig. 195) from the Tyrol
 cemeteries and ‘feeding cups’ from Griinwald and Lower
 Austria.
    The small vases are generally very fine and often blackened Graphite
 with graphite. Forms 2-5 are frequently ornamented with in- and
 cised patterns—zigzags, triangles, inverted wolf’s teeth—in a fluted
 broad belt framed by horizontal incisions and running round ornament
 the shoulder. The field may be broken up by four small
conical warts.+ Other vases, and especially the miniature Villa-
 nova urns, often show vertical or oblique flutings chiefly on
 the shoulder.’ Wart decoration is not uncommon.°
    Besides vases, the people of this group were apparently Fire-dogs
 making the so-called ‘moon-figures’ out of clay even in this
phase (cf. Fig. 152). The early examples come from settle-
ments along the Danube,’ but later they spread all over
Switzerland and down the Rhine. They seemed to have
served as fire-dogs, but are none the less certainly related
in the last resort to the Cretan Horns of Consecration*® and
the system of religious conceptions embodied in the steer
cult... The hearth was sacred, and these cult symbols mark
its sanctity.
   Next to the pottery the bronze ornaments best define our
group, since the arms belong to the types already enumerated.
The massive bracelets are twisted or heavily ribbed as Ez,
F3, and F3.

           twisted bow in several graves round Innsbruck, A3 a at Griin-

   Girdle     The girdle was fastened with the very distinctive clasp
    clasps type  3.2. The leather was studded with hollow buttons that
           now have loops on the inside, and perhaps wheel-pendants
           were hung from it. In the Griinwald and the Tyrolese urn-
      The fields the disk-pendants were already surmounted with heraldic
Hallstatt birds’ heads, a motive that has been rightly connected with the
      bir Egyptian winged disk probably in its later Hittite-Assyrian
           form.
  Chains      Chains of closed rings united by band-shaped links are also
           found in graves of the group (cf. Fig. 203).
           (b) The Knoviz Group.

 Canni-    bones and other kitchen refuse.? Matiegka many years ago
  balism   pointed out that the human bones, generally quite fragmentary,
           had been scorched by fire, split to extract the marrow, and
           hacked about with knives; they could then only be the remains
           of cannibal feasts.’? Matiegka’s inference has been confirmed
           by further observations in Bohemia, and the gruesome custom

has been also detected in Austria! The pottery and bronzes
from Knoviz deposits approximate so closely to those from
late Milavec barrows in Western Bohemia that the two groups
may here be treated as one.”
   Besides the urn with cylindrical neck, the biconical Lausitz Lausitz
ossuary (I) is common in Bohemia but generally in a rounded forms
variant. ‘he second Lausitz urn-form also occurs, usually
with a very long neck. In the later phase, E, it grows into the
‘storeyed urn’ (Etagenurne) with a bulging neck. The type is Storeyed
commonest      round Plzen,3 but also occurs on the Elbe, both urns
in Bohemia‘ and in Saxony,5 and sporadically even in Ober-
pfalz° and Franconia.’
   Other forms reminiscent of Lausitz B are fluted cups (like
Fig. 197a) and dishes with fluted rims (like Fig. 182a). The
Lausitz amphora is represented by a few stray examples with
degenerate warts.’ The pillar urns (Fig. 191) and the wide Pillar
bowls (Fig. 197c) have closer parallels farther west.           urns
   The smaller vessels, including the storeyed urns, are always Graphit-
smooth and not infrequently graphited in the later phases. ing
The big urns, on the other hand, are generally rough. Decora-
tion when present is restricted to fluting or light grooving.
Warts are rarely used, but a deep groove running round the
base of the neck of some late urns is taken over from the older
tumulus vases.
   The weapons of the Knoviz culture correspond to those
used farther west with the addition of a few Hungarian mace-
heads,’ like Plate 15, 3, imported from the south. But from
Plzen comes a remarkable bronze shield °—the earliest dated Shields
bronze shield from Central Europe (Plate 15). The type might
be regarded as a native development of the round wood or
leather shield of the tumulus culture, but has very close
parallels on the ninth-century Assyrian sculptures, to say
nothing of analogues from Great Britain and Scandinavia.

Bracelets      The ornaments, on the other hand, divergesubstantially from
            those current in Bavaria. Massive bracelets are found only in
            hoards;* light wire or ribbon types alone are found in Knoviz
     Pins   graves.   So, too, the pins ABo, Bro, E8, and D1 agree more
            closely with the Lausitz than the Austro-Bavarian series.
 Fibulae    Fibulae are found almost exclusively in hoards, and all in
            phase E. The commonest are the giant variants of B3. In the
            hoard of JenSovice? the several stages of manufacture may be
            seen—quadrangular bronze bar, bar hammered out flat in the
            middle, oval plate terminating in wires not yet coiled, and the
            article complete but for pin. Later still, spectacle fibulae, built
            on the plan of the Nordic series with a separate, pivoted pin,
            are found in the barrows round Plzen.
               Still a number of western types occur such as armlets or
            anklets like Fig. 170, 2, finger rings, like type 2 save that the
            wire of the spiral disks is twisted, compound neck-rings like
            Pl. 8, G2, razors of types 1-2, knives like type 9, &c. Armlets
            like narrow versions of B1, on the other hand, are a link with the
            eastern group.

           (c) Franconian Group.
           A small but highly specialized group of cemeteries in the basin
           of the Upper Main and Piegnitz must be mentioned here for
           the sake of completeness. ‘They are not urnfields, but are
           more closely related to the local ‘Tumulus cultures.
              Several methods of disposing of the corpse were in use
           concurrently. Besides inhumation in the extended position
   Secon- there are cases of secondary burial3 (interment after the flesh

           has decayed away from the bones), possible instances of roast-
           ing of the remains, the bronzes also showing traces of the
           action of fire? and cremations with or without inurnment of
  Crema- the ashes.’ The bones or cinerary urn lay either on a circular
      tion

    Circle surrounded by a similar stone bedding.       Sometimes a real

            and in some cases, even when a barrow was observed, it may
           have grown up as a result of successive secondary interments
           as Wunder suggested.7

derived from the Hercynian tumulus cultures and the eastern
and western groups of urnfields. Typologically they denote
two distinct periods, the first of which is the immediate con-
tinuation of the Middle Bronze Age tumulus culture.
   Apart from socketed arrow-heads, weapons are rare. On Knives
the other hand knives and razors are very abundant. The
typologically early forms of knife (2, 5, and 6) are so common
here that Kraft seems inclined to regard Franconia as their
original home. So, too, the primitive looking razors’ are so Razors
plentiful that they might have been differentiated in this area
though their ultimate home was Italy.
   In Reinecke’s Hallstatt A phase regular knives of Swiss (13
and 14)? and Upper Rhenish (10)3 types were introduced
apparently up the Main. From the same quarter came penannu-
lar razors,‘ ‘pendants’ of the same shape (as in the Lake-
dwellings), and the curious transverse celt B5a (Mihlanger)
of Swiss type.
   Among the ornaments twisted bracelets like ABr and F%3,
disks with a loop on the back, pins like D1 may be due to
eastern connexions, while anklets® are Rhenish and the vase-                             Anklets
headed pin from a late grave at Grundelsheim’ and perhaps
the commoner early pin D6 are Bavarian. A remarkable Amber
necklace of amber and blue glass beads from Henfenfeld illus- Glass
trates connexions with the north and south.
   The commonest ceramic form is a big urnlike vase with Urns
trumpet mouth (Fig. 198,a).* Later specimens? are taller
and more piriform. Such urns have analogues and perhaps
prototypes in the Late Bronze Age barrows and urnfields of
Upper Bavaria where pin D6 also occurs,” but might never-
theless be grouped with the North Alpine class. Parallels to
the later form can be cited from inhumation graves in ‘Thu-
ringia." But with this urn appear degenerate amphorae of the Lausitz
Hercynian tumulus type (Fig. 198, b, d),” and what might pass types
for very rounded descendants of the Lausitz ossuary (Fig.

            348             THE   NORTH     ALPINE    URNFIELDS
            198,c). In the Main valley Lausitz forms certainly occur.
            For instance, at Reindorf near Lichtenfels," an amphora, a cup,
            anda pot of the Lausitz B types, designated 4, 6, and 7on p. 322,
            were found in a cemetery of inhumation and cremation graves
            belonging perhaps to Reinecke’s Bronze Age D. Farther down
            stream at Fuchstadt a Lausitz amphora adorned with four
            groups of concentric circles? was found in a cremation grave
            under a barrowbelonging to Reinecke’s Hallstatt A. The closest
            parallels to this vase come not from the Lausitz but from
            Western Bohemia,} whence the two storeyed urns, mentioned on
            p- 345, must have been imported about the same time.
               Hence in Franconia we seem to have a survival of the
            Hercynian tumulus culture, crossed with an early northward
            push from the North Alpine urnfield culture and overlaid by
            a current from North-Western Bohemia.

                                      IV.   CONCLUSION
  Econo-    The group of urnfield cultures best illustrated in the Austro-
     mics   Bavarian cemeteries belong to a race of farmers who were not
            only successful cultivators but also possessed herds and horses.
            They were further engaged in trade and industry.
Sociology      They are marked out as a sedentary folk by their substantial
            villages and extensive cemeteries. he fortifications imply a
            close social organization, the great wealth of some graves sug-
            gests submission to princes or chiefs. War and fighting were
            common events in their lives. The whole impression of the
            period—the numerous hoards, the care lavished on weapons
            and defence indicate an age of struggle and unrest.‘
    Origin     It is generally held that the appearance of the urnfields
            denotes the advent of a new ethnic element in the Upper
            Danube basin. Every one agrees that the urnfield folk with
            their primarily agricultural and industrial economy, their closely
            knit communities and their peculiar burial rites, cannot be
Relations identified with the people of the tumuli.      These actually lived
to Tumu- in the highlands while the urnfield folk occupied the valleys,
   lus cul- and naturally an interchange of cultural elements took place
       ture
            between the two groups. We have noted already that many
            of the bronze and ceramic types found in the urnfields also
            mann has connected the vase with the   3 Barrow 98, at Haj, M. Plzen, no.
            Lausitz series.                        528.
            * M. Wirzburg.                         4 Cf. Reinecke, AuhV. v, p. 397.
            ? Behrens, fig. 40.
                                 c

Fic. 197.   Knoviz cups and dishes, Bubenic.   Narodni Museum.
                          aand bi,ct




                              c                         d
       b
   Fic. 198. Urns and amphorae, Henfenfeld.       M. Nurnberg
                THE NORTH ALPINE URNFIELDS                            349
occur in the contemporary barrows of Upper Bavaria. The
bronze-hilted swords derived from type B3, perhaps also the
taste for heavily ribbed pins and bracelets, would have been
borrowed from the Bavarian tumulus-builders. On the other
hand, the fine pottery from the later barrows is far more likely
to have been the work of the sedentary peasants than of the
pastoral hill folk whose metalwork was always superior to
their pottery. The relations between the Knoviz folk of Central
Bohemia and the Milave¢ people of the west, who had ad-
vanced as far east as Prague (Velka Dobra), would be of the
same order. The contact between the two groups in either
case can hardly have been always peaceful, and probably it is
their opposition which gives the peculiarly martial character to
the North Alpine urnfield cultures. At the same time, while
a section of the tumulus folk kept apart, another section, both
 in Bohemia and Bavaria, may well have been absorbed in or
 have dominated the urnfield folk and come to constitute an
 integral and distinctive part of the new communities.”
    It remains possible that the urnfield folk were (I) descen-
 dants of the local Aunjetitz-Straubing population of the low-
 lands, or (II) invaders (a) from the Lausitz area or (6) from
 the south-east, or were (III) constituted from a combination of
 all these elements. Their culture certainly shows points of
 agreement with each of the three groups enumerated.
    (I) The urnfields occupy very much the same area as the Relations
 Early Bronze Age cemeteries, and in some cases (Kelheim, to
 Straubing, Kornwestheim, Gemeinlebarn) the same site was Aunjetitz
 occupied in both periods. At the last-named site a definite cultures
 continuity between the inhumation and cremation graves has
 been asserted by Hoernes,’ and a curious blending of Lausitz,
 North Hungarian (Egyek), and North Alpine types is notice-
able. Moreover, the urnfield cultures are the economic heirs
of the Aunjetitz, as farther east. Nor is the contrast in burial
rites complete. We have contracted skeletons at Gemeinlebarn.
Conversely, a cremation burial with pottery of the Straubing
type is reported from Kelheim.3
   The distinctive urn with cylindrical neck is regarded by
most authorities as derived in the last resort from Aunjetitz
forms.? The intermediate types have, however, not yet been
traced either in Bohemia or Lower Bavaria, but might well be
1 Cf. Cervinka’s remarks on the Kno- _ bathy.
viz culture, Real. vii, p. 11.         3 p. 235 above.
2 JZK. i, p. 43; it is denied by Szom- 4 Kraft, BY. 131, pp. 168 f.
            350              THE    NORTH      ALPINE    URNFIELDS
          sought in Lower Austria or Slovakia. In the same way the
          jugs of the Austro-Bavarian group may be connected with
          Straubing forms, but come nearer Moravian and Lower Aus-
          trian Aunjetitz shapes. The fine black ware of Bavaria is like-
          wise reminiscent of the Eastern Aunjetitz fabrics. A survival
          of the Aunjetitz population might then be admitted both in
          Bohemia and Lower Bavaria, but to explain the change a new
          element would have to be postulated.
Theory of |The theory of an incursion of Lausitz people has found
a Lausitz more supporters.    The Czech school‘ explain the Knoviz cul-
incursion   ture as a westward extension of the Lausitz culture, crossed
         with Aunjetitz survivors. Cervinka* rightly insists on the
         contribution made by outposts of the West Bohemian tumulus
         culture. An extension of the same wave from the Lausitz to
         Bavaria has been favoured by many German authorities.
  Agree-    The agreements between the two cultures are certainly
   ments striking. In addition to a general community of economic
 between life (agriculture, industry, trade) and social structure (regular
     two village-communities) we have a perfect agreement in funeral
  groups    rites—cremation, inurnment of the ashes, burial in urnfields.
            This agreement extends to details. The typical Lausitz ossuary
            is found in Western          Bohemia,     Lower     Austria, the Tyrol,
            Bavaria, and even the Rhineland.            In two or three cases even
            a ‘Seelenloch’ was provided (in urns of native type). The
            domestic architecture (log-cabin building) and control of the
            horse again serve to connect the western province to the
            eastern.
               In the Knoviz area quite a number of accessory vases and
            even the bronzes (especially the pins) belong to Lausitz types;
            farther west only the cups look at all Lusatian, the ornaments
            are essentially different.
Contrasts      Despite the many significant points of agreement, the North
            Alpine and Lausitz urnfields are separated by contrasts that
            are scarcely less important. The Lausitz graves are always
            very poor in bronzes; the North Alpine sometimes quite rich;
            the Lausitz culture looks peaceful and democratic; the North
            Alpine definitely martial and aristocratic; the Lausitz settle-
            ments were never fortified; the sites in Bavaria and Tyrol were
            chosen definitely with a view to defence. Perhaps these diver-
            gences might be explained by the circumstances of the Lausitz
            invaders in a country occupied by the warlike barrow-builders.
            I Buchtela, JZEK. iv, p. 32; Schranil,   > Real. vii, p. 11.
            Béhmen, p. 148; Stocky, BAB., p. 23.     3 Mannus, Erganzbd. v, p. 52, n. 2.
                 THE NORTH        ALPINE     URNFIELDS                     aet
   That will explain some of the differences in industry, too.
Others seem more deep seated. The Lausitz people used a
socketed celt; in the urnfield area west of the Elbe this type
is rare; the regular axe belongs to the family of winged celts.
The typical ossuary is almost foreign to the Lausitz culture.’
 _ The contrast is accentuated by the existence of a vague fron- The Elbe
tier along the Elbe in Bohemia: on the east lie the extensive a frontier
cemeteries of poor graves with a pure Lausitz inventory, on
the west the burials in urns with cylindrical necks and rich
hoards containing Austro-Bavarian types (Paseka). A mass
expansion of the Lausitz culture to Bavaria, such as we can
trace in Moravia, is inconceivable.         None the less a real incur-
sion of Lausitz people seems required to explain the wide
distribution of the typical ossuary. The only question is the
extent and importance of this element as a constitutive factor
in the new unity.
   Kraft ? has enumerated several cultural elements in the South South-
German urnfields which have their rootsin Hungary or the south- Eastern
east. These—clay plastic, theriomorphs, pintaderas, rattles— elements
have already been found as accidental elements in the Lausitz
culture.3 That the ‘horn-shaped’ fire-dogs have the same ante-
cedents seems probable, but cannot be proved in view of the
questionable date of the Hungarian specimens, e.g. from Len-
gyel.t The ‘experienced miners’ postulated by Kyrle5 could
in Central Europe only be found in Slovakia or Hungary, and
the bronze technique disclosed in the urnfields was essentially
Hungarian. Perhaps the Soroksar type of urn (Fig. 155) may
have contributed to the formation of the pillar-urns. Other-
wise the Hungarian influence might be accounted for by ex-
ternal relations attested for the end of the Middle Bronze Age
by the cylinders and pendants from Oberpfalz, and later by
the Hungarian swords from the Tyrol and Bavaria and the
mace-heads from Bohemia.            On the other hand, the influence
of the Hungarian bronze culture on the frontier civilizations
of Lower Austria went deeper. We have, for instance, types of
the Tészeg-Egyek group at Gemeinlebarn (Fig. 178c). The
conversion of the urn with cylindrical neck into an ossuary
might well have taken place in that region. The fine fluted
ware is richly developed there, and might even have grown up

        in Austria out of a fusion between survivals of the Baden type
        with Aunjetitz elements. It is therefore tempting to bring a
        substantial element of the new population from this quarter.
        This explanation might be combined with I a if it be admitted
        that Lausitz settlers had reached Lower Austria at the begin-

        was parallel to rather than dependant on that of Lausitz
        proper.
           We incline to consider the new factor in the urnfield cul-
        tures of Bavaria to be due to the amalgam of late Aunjetitz,
        Gata, and Lausitz elements created in Lower Austria. A move-
        ment of the new composite group towards Bohemian tin and
          Alpine copper, bringing it under the influence of the Bohemian
          and Bavarian tumulus cultures respectively, would have re-
          sulted in the formation of the two groups just described.
  Italian    In the urnfields we meet from the start a series of accidental
influence forms generally regarded as of Mediterranean or rather Adriatic
          origin. That attribution is patently correct in the case of the
          glass beads, the Peschiera fibulae from the Tyrol,” and perhaps
          even the looped type from Griinwald.3 The southern origin of
          the razors and bronze vessels is less obvious. The early razors
          from Oberpfalz and Bohemia might rank as a local invention
          did they not sometimes betray the indent that links them in
        an irreversable manner to the Italian and Sicilian series
                                                                ;+ the
        later penannular type A4 is probably a native development
        despite a few parallels from Upper Italy.5 Bronze cups re-
        sembling the North Alpine specimens in shape first occur in
        the hoard at Tolfa® but, as Reinecke 7 remarks, there is no
        reason why such hammered work should not have been pro-
        duced north of the Alps. As a matter of fact neither the smooth
        cups nor the wheeled bowls can be matched in Italy. The
        knife dagger, Plate I, B4, is a late Mycenaean type ® but again

occurs at both ends of the Apennine Peninsula.t It may be
presumed, too, that the revival of spiral ornamentation illus-
trated on the sword-hilts was due to Mycenaean influence, and
here again the Adriatic * must have been the mediator.
   Relations at this time were not one-sided. In Upper Italy North
we find pins of types D1, D11, &c.,3 wheel-pendants,? twisted Alpine
torques,’ and other types that seem certainly to have come type in
from beyond the Alps and serve in a general way to confirm Italy
the dating of the Late Bronze Age suggested on p. 255.
   We cannot at the moment go further and point to any North
Alpine group as the ancestor of the Villanova or proto-Villa-
nova (Pianello) culture. Despite the parallelism of the bronze
industries and frequent interchange of their products only the
early Alban cemeteries include pottery that looks at all ‘North
Alpine’. As a matter of fact it is the Comacine culture rather
than the more easterly groups round Bologna and Este that
show most affinity to the North Alpine area.

                                                  XVIII
                       FROM        SWITZERLAND                TO BRITAIN
                              I. THE    LAST      LAKE-DWELLINGS
  Bronze      N Switzerland the inhabitants of the pile-villages on the lake
     Age       shores had ‘preserved their old neolithic culture long after
    lake-  bronze was in regular use in other parts of Europe’.*’ A few
dwellings Early and Middle Bronze Age objects are found in many lake-
           dweilings which are usually designated ‘neolithic’ because of
           the immense preponderance of stone implements.
   Ceme-      On the other hand, in the valleys several cemeteries, fur-
    teries nished with Middle Bronze Age objects, have come to light
           that bear no visible relation to any lacustrine settlements.?
           Those in North-Eastern Switzerland are mainly interments
           under barrows furnished with bronzes proper to the Swabian
           group of the Tumulus Culture. In the valleys of the Rhone and
           Aar,3 on the other hand, the graves are small cists or simple
           trenches without any barrow over them. They are furnished
           with spatuliform celts (Fig. 199), triangular (A1) and bronze
           hilted (Az) daggers, and later short ogival swords (PI. I, A6),
           and other objects of ‘western’ type; even halberd blades occur
           sporadically. Kraft+ holds that these types are derived in
           the last resort from the El Argar culture of Spain, but that the
           bronze-hilted daggers represent a local development. At the
           same time he admits that many of the ornaments from the
           graves or found stray in the same region, such as ingot-torques
             and pins Ar, A2 (one stray), A3, A4, A6, and Ao, are of eastern,
            i.e. Hungarian or Bohemian, origin. In fact the group, which
            only begins about phase B of the Bronze Age, is like the Alsa-
            tian, primarily dependent upon the Danubian bronze industry.
            It none the less developed a distinct individuality of its own,
            and may be designated after Kraft the Rhone culture.
 The urn-      The advent of the urnfield folk transformed Alpine civiliza-
  field in- tion. Instead of the multiplicity of small hamlets that had
    vasion lined the shore of every lake in the
                                                  ‘Stone-Age’, a few much
            larger settlements5 were built, generally farther out from the
Synoicism present shore. These are the lake-dwellings traditionally de-
    of lake scribed as Bronze Age, which nevertheless belong in fact to

Reinecke’s Hallstatt A (our Late Bronze Age E) and the suc-
ceeding period. Precisely similar settlements were established
on some Bavarian lakes (Starnberg and the Federseemoor), and
perhaps in the bed of the Rhine.
   The position of the new villages, far from the shore, has Low level
been explained by Reinerth and Vouga as due to the lower of lakes
level of the lake waters at the height of the Subboreal dry
epoch. The replacement of a multitude of small hamlets by a
few substantial villages seems to have been the result of a
synoicism effected by the urnfield folk who, as we have seen,
reached the Aar already in the first half of the Late Bronze Age.
  In any case it is certain that the new epoch was ushered in Change
by the advent of a new people. Franchet" has pointed out in pottery

that the Late Bronze Age pottery of Switzerland marks a com-
plete break with neolithic traditions. The pottery is now fine
and evenly fired;wood and stone tools are used instead of bone
in its manufacture; the clay is allowed to dry before ornaments
are incised in it.
   The forms include many types belonging to the urnfield Forms
cultures, notably the compound vases, feeding cups, therio-
morphs (Figs. 200-2), small ‘Villanovan’ pots with fluted
ornament? (like Fig. 195), urns with cylindrical necks and
shallow dishes (Fig. 201). Some elements in the decoration
are patently derived directly from the urnfield cultures, whereas
others, e.g., the common fretwork motives (Fig. 202c), come Fretwork
from the Tumulus group or from the Rhone culture. No less ornament
valuable clues as to the origin of the new element are provided
by the numerous ‘moon idols’ (horn-shaped fire-dogs) (Fig. Fire-dogs

knives like 9 and 11, the wheel pendants and the universal
pins with miniature vase-heads, while the distinctive globe-

New do- horse which first appears at this time,’ and the horn-ended

   Log- shelters previously used.”                                ae
  cabins      The Swiss ‘Bronze Age’ that arose on this foundation, if
           rather belated, was original and creative. ‘The superposition
           of the urnfield folk upon the older lake-dwellers formed an
           industrious and ingenious population, whose energies were
           stimulated by contact with remote regions and the proximity
           of the Villanova culture of Upper Italy.
 Metal-      The lake-dwellers were, of course, cultivators               and stock-
   lurgy   breeders, but in the Late Bronze Age they blossomed forth,
           above all, as skilled smiths and traders. Every village had its
           bronze workshop,} the remains of which are marked by in-
           numerable moulds of clay or sandstone, hammers, ingots of
          copper, tin, and bronze, and fragments of the clay pipes which
Speciali- supplied the blast to the furnaces. Moreover, there is evidence
  sation for regular specialization at this time, since any given type,
          common to all the lake villages, is found in each case in
          greatest profusion only at one of them.‘
‘Foreign     The products of these expert craftsmen found a market in
   trade’ quite distant lands. So the distinctive Swiss type of knife (14)
          has been found in Silesia and Hungary, and many other widely
          distributed forms whose origin is to-day less certain, such as
          antennae and Ronzano swords, may well be of Swiss manu-
          facture. At the same time the lake-dwellers seem to have
          secured control of the amber route down the Rhine as well as
          the west-to-east traffic in French, and perhaps even Cornish,
Imports    tin. Many exotic products—tin, lead, gold, amber, jet, and
    from   glass—and foreign artifacts are found in the lake-dwellings.
Scandi-    Even bronze ‘basins’ and fibulae manufactured in Scandinavia
   navia   reached Western Switzerland.s Naturally Italian manufac-
Villano-   tures were well represented. The fragment of a bronze bit of
     van   Benacci I type from Alpenquai, Zurich (Fig. 203, 2), is a fine
 imports   instance.
Weights       This great commercial activity naturally involved the use
           of some system of weights. In 1907 Robert Forrer recognized
           that certain roughly spherical or hemispherical lumps of lead
           (or stone) provided with bronze loops for suspension were
           weights. They correspond to multiples of various early East

Mediterranean weights—the Aeginetan, Phoenician, Egyptian
(Italic) or Carthaginian mina respectively." Certain types of
ring may have served as currency.
   The enumeration of all the bronze types current would be Bronzes
tedious. The more important only need be mentioned. By
far the commonest celts are the type with wings near the butt Winged
and a loop at the side including specimens with a transverse celts
blade like Fig. 203, 11.     Socketed celts are quite rare, but
socketed chisels are as common as winged celts.
  The knives include many forms such as Io, 12, and 13, Knives
but 14 alone -is distinctively Swiss and indubitably a local
form. The commonest razor is the penannular form, but Razors
single-bladed crescents are also found (Fig. 203, 4).
   The favourite weapons were antennae or Ronzano swords, Swords
which may have been manufactured locally.
   Of the innumerable pins, that with a great hollow globular Pins
head ornamented with holes encircled by engraved rings (E11)
was a distinctive local type. Fibulae are far from common.
The great majority, and particularly the ribbed form (A’2 a),
seem Italian imports.
   A speciality of West Switzerland was a thick, hollow armlet
of penannular form adorned with concentric circles united by
parallel lines.
  Rings, wheels, lunulae, &c., served as pendants.
   Stone and horn were now scarcely used for implements. Bits
Horn cheek-pieces for bits were not uncommon. Besides these
occur bronze bits of various forms; one very primitive speci-
men hasa rigid snaffle bar. It quite possibly represents a local
translation into metal of the older type with a wooden or
leather snaffle.
   Though older types naturally occur, the Bronze Age lake- Relative
dwellings appear to begin in the latter half of the Late Bronze date
Age (Reinecke’s Hallstatt A). The lower limit for their exis-
tence is supplied not so much by occasional iron objects as by
sherds of Hallstatt B(C) pottery painted in black and red or in
graphite on a matt black ground.’ Alpenquai and many other
sites were abruptly deserted after this, but some villages on
the western lakes may have survived the catastrophe. The
regular Bronze Age lake-dwellings do not, however, long
outlast the first third of the first Iron Age (Reinecke Hall-
statt B).

                               II. THE     RHENISH      URNFIELDS
           The urnfield cultures spread rapidly all down the Rhine, reach-
           ing Holland and Northern Belgium during the second phase of
Intrusive the Late Bronze Age. The expansion into the Rhine valley
       and may well have begun already in the earlier phase (Reinecke D),
   native and doubtless took place in several waves and very likely by
 cultures several routes, just as had the original colonization by the
           Danubians in neolithic times. At the same time the urnfield
           folk encountered not only tumulus-builders as in Bavaria, but
           very likely also remnants of the Adlerberg population still
           living in the valleys. Hence the Rhenish urnfields, despite
           many common traits,‘ present on closer examination a very
           diversified aspect. Kraft5 has very effectively emphasized
           this diversity and sought to unravel the complex events that
           caused it. He recognizes two main currents: an earlier group
           in phase D, coming from Upper Italy and characterized by
           swords like A3 (in a special variant) and pins like Eg, and a
           later with swords of series C, and pins like Ero.
    North     In the last chapter we mentioned several cemeteries from
   Alpine the Upper Rhine and from the Neckar containing bronze and
      urns ceramic types distinctive of the North Alpine urnfields. The
           northernmost point known to the author is Rheingénheim in

profoundly influenced the culture of the natived Rhenish
tumulus-builders, especially those who, like the tribes living
round Haguenau,” dwelt right on the borders of the good agri-
cultural land occupied by the urnfield folk.
   On the Middle Rhine, especially on the west bank (Palati- Crema-
nate and Rheinhessen), but also in Oberhessen, there are several tions

Reinecke’s phase D. Characteristic of these graves are double- urns

and shepherd’s crook-pins. The pottery includes jugs and

represent the survival of a native tradition; in the latter influ- with
ences from Bavaria are perhaps detectable. Since in some Adlerberg
cases the graves in question actually occur in cemeteries already culture
used by the Early Bronze Age Adlerberg folk (e.g. on the
Adlerberg itself), it might be argued that they belong to de-
scendants of that people. The burial rite and the bronzes none
the less reveal the influence of the urnfield cultures of Kraft’s
‘North Italian’ group.

Rhine (extending as far as the Sieg), on the Neckar, and on group
the Main from the vicinity of Wiirzburg down stream, contain
a very distinctive furniture.

urn or dolion. The dolion is rough and ornamented, if at all,

          but quite as often (II) the wide rim, distinctive of that group
          on the Danube or the Elbe, is omitted,’ both variants occurring
          side by side. In a third variant (III) the body is angular and
          definitely biconical, while even the neck is slightly conical.”
          Finally, we find cases in which the open jar, such as we saw at
          Eisenbichl near Reichenhall (Fig. 192 b), is used as an urn.3
Ossuary      The ossuary proper and the accessory vases are of a very
          fine fabric, generally dark faced and ornamented with horizontal
          facets, grooves, fine incisions or, rarely, bosses. The angular
          profiles, broad faceted rims and stepped shoulders, are un-
          mistakable. The ossuary has a slightly conical neck and recalls
         the Villanova form in general outline, though the neck is rela-
         tively shorter than in Upper Italy (Fig. 204c). On the Main
         vases of this type are sometimes provided with two handles at
         the base of the neck when they remotely resemble Lausitz
         amphorae‘ (Fig. 204a). Variants with a single handle are also
         known 5 (Fig. 204b).
            The remaining vases from graves are small variants of the
         ossuary and dishes with wide faceted rims and small bases
         (Fig. 204d). Rattles and feeding-bowls occur sporadically.
Weapons     Graves of this group are furnished sparingly with weapons
         (antennae and Ronzano swords, socketed arrow- and spear-
         heads). Celts are represented only by rare stone specimens.®
         Penannular razors and a large assortment of knives are more
         common.
  Orna-     The ornaments include compound collars (G2) as at Griin-
   ments wald, globe-head pins (E10), including pure Swiss types (E11),’
          and those with a miniature vase-head, and two-membered
         fibulae types B3* and B4.. Big anklets of type 3 Fig. 170 have
         been obviously taken over from the Tumulus culture. Glass
    Gold beads and gold are rare. The precious metal, however, occurs
         in the form of rings of type 1a and of disks stamped with groups
         of concentric circles. Finally, we may note several bronze cups.
   Flat-    Pottery and bronzes of the types just described were usually
  graves found in urnfields, but precisely the same material occurs

sometimes under barrows.’ Far more irregular are flat inhu-
mation graves containing a similar furniture and sometimes
actually forming part of an urnfield. Often such inhumation Inhuma-
graves are peculiarly rich. At Wollmesheim in the Palatinate? tions
one grave contained the skeletons of a man and his wife, urns
of variant III, a sword of type C4, arrow-heads, two anklets
of type 3, a fibula of type B4, and other bronzes. Near by
were two cremation interments in urns, accompanied by the
same types of anklet and fibula as the skeletons.
   This group is generally treated as a mere development of Relations
the North Alpines group under the influence of Italian and with
Swiss metallurgy. The form and ornamentation of the faceted North
pottery in particular would be due to the imitation of metal Alps
forms.3 Kraft, on the other hand, would recognize here a
cross between the North Alpine groups and currents from the
Lausitz and Bohemia coming down the Main. This stream and with
would have brought with it the ceramic fluting from which Lausitz
the facets evolved, certain pot-forms, and the two-membered
fibulae.s The occurrence of a biconical ossuary of Lausitz
form® at Weisenheim in the Palatinate is certainly favourable
to the idea.     So too, the amphorae, like Fig. 203a, from the
Main cemeteries might be regarded as descendants of the
Lausitz amphora.
   If, however, we look farther east to the Upper Weser basin Fulda
we see that round Fulda the urnfield culture is inspired rather shows
from the south-west than from the east. The urns belong for Lausitz
the most part to the Rhenish types I,’ III,> (and IV), and influence
are associated with very late vase-head pins, a degenerate small
variant of pin E11 with much reduced head, and perhaps even
bits of iron. The cemeteries therefore produce a definite im-
pression of lateness as contrasted with those on the Rhine or
Main.    Hence, though ossuaries, having the form of a Lausitz
amphora, do occur,’ we cannot regard these late remains as
favourable to the. idea of West Lausitz prototypes for the
Rhenish urnfield culture. If a strong current from the east
were affecting the latter we should expect to find evidence for
it first in a place like Fulda, whereas the contrary is the case.

             We incline, therefore, to think that Kraft has over-estimated
             the direct Lausitz influence on the Rhine.
                A detailed account of the urnfield culture on the Middle
             Rhine is superfluous, since it reproduces almost precisely what
             we have already described in the Upper Danube basin and
             Switzerland. All the main traits noted there—regular villages,
             agriculture, industry and trade, and peculiarities of detail such
             as the horned fire-dogs '—recur farther north, albeit in a poorer
    Trade    form. The distribution of the many hoards* and their con-
             tents disclose trade routes from the north from the Upper
             Weser basin across the Wetterau to the Lower Main, and from
             the west via the Moselle as well as from the east up the Danube
             to the Upper Rhine and across the Furth gap to the Upper
             Main.
   Amber      The first route brought, with amber, objects of Scandinavian
           type such as the plate fibulae and ‘suspension basins’ similar to
Gold from those that we have already met in Switzerland.2 Many of the
      west gold objects, particularly the remarkable ‘hat’ from Schiffer-
           stadt in the Palatinate 4 (exactly like one from Avanton in the
           Department of Vienne), and perhaps also the numerous gold
           ‘sun-disks’ ornamented with stamped circles, would be made
           of French (or Irish) gold.’ Hungarian and Bohemian types,
           apart from those brought with them naturalized by the urn-
    Swiss field people, are, on the other hand, rare. The main inspira-
 influence tion of the Rhenish industry came from Switzerland (pins like
             D4, Exo, hollow armlets with circle ornaments,® penannular
             razors, antennae and Ronzano swords, &c.), and through
             Switzerland from Italy (Villanova forms in the pottery).
                The urnfield cultures may have developed for a long time in
             the valleys side by side with the remains of the old 'Tumulus
             culture in the highlands of Wurtemberg, Baden, the Oden-
             wald, and the Taunus. Clearly it lasted well into the Iron
             Age proper.
Intrusion       Eventually it came under the influence of the Hallstatt

necke* has made into a special phase, B, of the Hallstatt period,

               III.   THE   LOWER        RHINE   AND     BRITAIN
On the Lower Rhine below Cologne genuine urnfields of the Barrows
South German type are no longer forthcoming. On the other and urn-
hand, the later tumuli approximate in many respects to urn- fields
fields and intrude into Holland (Limburg and Brabant) and
Belgium.      The burial rite is always cremation; the ashes, en-
closed in an ossuary, are buried in shallow pits dug in virgin
soil; the barrows are very low and cluster together to form
regular cemeteries. Dutch and Belgian archaeologists in fact
always term these late groups of barrows urnfields.
   These modifications of the earlier burial rites suggest an
admixture of urnfield folk with the old barrow-builders, and
the grave goods confirm that impression.
   The commonest urn is a bulging type with short cylindrical Urn
that should probably be considered a descendant of the Rhenish
type II, though Rademacher regards it as native.* The angular
urn of type III is less common but is, however, known even in
Holland.5 The open urn type IV also occurs.°
   Broad rimmed dishes and small pots of Villanovan form like
Fig. 1957 also occur.
   The ware is generally brownish in colour, and the southern Native
ornamentation by fluting or grooving is rarely seen. On the elements
other hand, the urns and accessory vases are frequently adorned in decora-

  Bronzes    "The Lower Rhenish urn-burials are practically without
           bronze furniture. We have at best a couple of knives like 123
           and a unique type from Wesel, pins with decadent poppy- or
           vase-heads,* tweezers, and looped buttons.’ A perforated stone
           axe was found in one grave at Riethoven, but without ceramic
           remains. A Ronzano sword found in the Waal® must have
           been brought to Holland by the urnfield folk.
 Chrono-      The poverty of bronzes makes a relative dating of the Lower
      logy Rhenish and Dutch cemeteries difficult. Holwerda’ assigns
           them boldly to the beginning of our era. Rademacher® and
           Stampfuss? date them a thousand years earlier. Actually the
           truth would lie between these two views.
   Endof      South of Dusseldorf and Duisburg the advance of the Hall-
 urnfields statt culture (Giindlingen ?) limits the urnfield phase below.”
             Farther north this southern influence is not felt, but the cul-
             ture of the type just described is eventually superseded by that
             which Holwerda terms ‘German’," the Harpstadt type of
             Stampfuss” representing a later wave of the same. The Ger-
             man, influence, characterized by biconical or open urns with
             finger-tip ornament on their walls and rims (instead of on
             raised strips) would, according to Rademacher,"3 have reached
             Holland about 500 B.c. An urn of this family in a late variant
             was found containing a Roman fibula of the first century a.D.,™
             but that circumstance does not oblige us to reduce Rade-
             macher’s figure for the beginning of the second or German
             phase in Holland materially.
Extension        The urnfield culture of the Lower Rhine therefore appears
to Britain   as a last outpost of the North Alpine group that had absorbed
             much of the tumulus population. But its roots in the south

               FROM     SWITZERLAND          TO BRITAIN                 365
were soon broken, so that it stagnated till overwhelmed by
Teutonic tribes from farther east. Its peculiar interest is that
the Lower Rhenish culture in its last stage crossed the sea to
Britain as the finds from Scarborough: show. As at the begin-
ning of the Bronze Age the beaker folk from the Upper Rhine,
mingled with the Battle-axe folk from Central Germany, in-
vaded Britain, so again at the end of that age the Urnfield
people from the east crossed the North Sea.

                                     XIX
  THE      END      OF THE HUNGARIAN BRONZE                            AGE
                       AND THE SCYTHS
IK Slovakia and the Hungarian plain the regular use of iron
   was not introduced from the south-west but from the east.
But the new metal was only adopted long after the original
Bronze Age civilization had been reduced to chaos by the in-
cursion of Lausitz folk fromthe north-west, and whena brilliant,
if belated, Bronze Age culture had arisen out of the fusion of
intrusive and autochthonous elements.
   We have already noted that the Lausitz people advanced The Lau-
in a compact mass as far as Centra] Slovakia.t But they cer- sitz inva-
tainly extended their domain much farther south, overrunning sion
the whole plain and even crossing the Balkans by the Morava—
Vardar road to make temporary settlements in Macedonia. The
results of this incursion were to remodel the boundaries of the
cultural provinces, sketched in Chapter XIV, and to alter their
civilization more or less profoundly.
   The current of invasion probably followed two routes. Its
effects west of the Danube are described in Chapter XX. The
changes in the plain and east concern us here. In the north
elements from our groups 1 and 5 were absorbed in a hybrid
culturein group I, in which the intruders were at first dominant.
Other elements from group 1 seem to have been brushed aside
westward, and people from group 5 were driven across the
Tisza to form a new compound, group II, with older Transyl-
vanian constituents. The southern settlements of group 17
and apparently the whole of group 2 vanish, while group 4
extends its domain to the Danube at Gerjen, to Syrmia, and
perhaps up the Maros as group III, while a IVth group is
vaguely discernible among the hills of ‘Transylvania.
               I. THE     NORTH      HUNGARIAN         GROUP
The composite group I occupies the whole zone fringing the
metalliferous mountains of Slovakia and North Hungary from
the Hron (Gran) and the Danube to the steppe country in
Hajdu County beyond the Tisza. From this area we have im-
I p. 324 above.                           Dunakeszi and Vatya, proving a partial
2 Nevertheless a few typical fluted       survival of these urnfields into Period
cups, as in Tészeg D, are found spora-    VI.
dically in cemeteries of group I, as at
          368        THE END      OF THE HUNGARIAN          BRONZE      AGE
          portant settlements, enormous urnfields, and a vast number of
         rich hoards. These evidently cover a long period beginning
         quite early in the Late Bronze Age and lasting well into our
         Period VII.
Villages    The settlements include ‘villages on hills like Pilin and
         ‘terremare’, like the upper strata at Tészeg, Tiszafiired, and
         Szihalom, while remains of the period are found in many caves
         that have also yielded Bukk ware.
            Some settlements have been excavated, but unfortunately
         no detailed reports are available. It is clear that defensible
         heights were chosen, as in the Tyrol, and some centres, e.g.
         Pilin, may have been fortified. The distribution of the largest
         villages round the metalliferous mountains suggests that they
  Metal were connected with metallurgy. Numerous moulds from
working Pilin,*     Aszdd,?     Fels6-Kubin,3    together    with    fragments      of
          blast-pipes and metallurgical tools, confirm this impression.
          As we shall see, the metal trade brought to the region a variety
          of foreign imports, while a multitude of quite distinctive local
          types were evolved and distributed over a wide area.
  Ceme-      The cemeteries were adjacent to the settlements, and in some
   teries cases two or more were attached to the same village; no less
          than six have been located in a small area round Hatvan.!
   Urns      In the cemeteries cremation was universal. The urns vary
          greatly in form; no two urns were alike in the twenty graves
          excavated at Zagyvapalfalva.s The most usual type is bulging
          or piriform, with a slightly conical neck and everted rim. There
          are often two or more small handles just under the belly (Fig.
          205, 2) or sitting on the shoulder as in ‘Silesian’ urns (Fig.
          206, b). More rarely the handles join the rim and shoulder in
          a manner reminiscent of Gata (Fig. 206, a). A less common
          type of urn is just a large open-mouthed pot like the Lausitz
          forms 7-8 (Fig. 205, 4 and 12). These last are left rough: the
          bulging urns are always smooth, dark-faced, and often graphited.
Decora-      An almost universal method of decoration was the applica-
     tion tion of small warts that may be framed by grooved
                                                                semicircles
          and combined with horizontal or vertical flutings, incised arcs
          or arcadings, and plastic rope mouldings as in the proto-
          Lausitz urn of Fig. 174.°
 Dishes     The urns were covered with dishes reminiscent of earlier
          z peeks pl. tv.                       5 AE. xl (1923-6), pp. 60 f.
          2   Ibid., pl. v.                     ° e.g. Zagyvapalfalva, AE. xl, fig. 22
          : AE. ii (1882), pp. 279 f.           (left).
            CIA. 1877, ii, p. 166.
                           AND THE SCYTHS                                    369
forms (Fig. 207), but not without parallels in the Lausitz
area. ‘Those in use at Egyek and Hajdu Bagos in particular
agree with the dishes of T’észeg C both in form and ornament
(Fig. 205, 8; cf. Fig. 163), but it will be noticed that the warts
are degenerate in comparison with the older specimens.
   The accessory vases include numerous Lausitz forms:                               Lausitz
amphorae,     fluted,’    or   even    wart-ornamented,?             cups   with forms
shoulder flutings (Fig. 205, ga), turban dishes 3 and pots of
Lausitz type 7-8.4 According to Pi¢,5 even a Lausitz ossuary
of type 2 was found in one of the cemeteries near Aszod.




                                            MANNMN TTTTTT TTT


            Fic. 205.    Pottery from Egyek and Hajdubagos.      3

   The pottery thus shows a combination of two traditions.
If many of the urns and the style of their decoration may be
descended from native Hungarian types, the intrusive element
is attested by the Lausitz forms just enumerated.®
   The graves are very poorly furnished with bronzes, and these Grave-
have an extraordinarily archaic look. Weapons are restricted to goods
atypical daggers, knives, and socketed arrow-heads, though
miniature palstavs, socketed celts, swords, and button sickles,
found at Pilin,’? may have been made for funerary use. The Pins
ornaments reveal a mixture of native types, already familiar in
the Middle Bronze Age of Hungary, and innovations that were

         at home in the Lausitz or Bohemia.           To the former group be-
         long, besides the simple shape F1, the bracelets B2,"* C2,3 D1
          (thin),? pins Ao (everywhere), C5a,3 E12,” B7,” *tutuli like Az,
          and Ao and pendants like type B3,3 developing into B4.” 3          ;
 Foreign     As foreign forms we may: reckon the twisted torque ABr,*
    types finger rings with double spiral ends,” 4 pins D4,° Bro,” * and a
          variant on C8,? and we may class the tweezers from Aszod and
          Zagyvapalfalva as intrusive too.
             A curious item in the furniture of certain graves at Zagyva-
          palfalva® was constituted by flat stone disks to which Felsé
          Kubin? and some Moravian urnfields offer parallels.
 Hoards      If the cemeteries illustrate the tenacity with which old
          native fashions were preserved in the teeth of all racial move-
          ments, the hoards illustrate the revolution caused by the
          Lausitz intrusion, the adaptability and wealth of some class at
          least in the new complex, and the extensive trade relations of
          North Hungary. The hoards in question occupy almost exactly
          the same area as the cemeteries and settlements just described,
          though they are particularly numerous, as might be expected,
          in the dangerous frontier regions beyond the Tisza. Though
          only a few types found in the cemeteries recur in the hoards,®
          there are no other connected remains to which the latter can be
          attached. And yet it is clear that many of the types they con-
          tain were created in North Hungary. Hence we must conclude
          that the people who made and used the objects from the
          depots were the same as those buried in the cemeteries, or
          at least a class among the latter.
Division    ~The hoards are not all contemporary. Though some are
     into useless for dating purposes, being mere collections of scrap
  phases metal, it is possible to divide the rest up between two or three
          phases, of which the last already falls certainly into Period VII.
  Supre-     The perforated axe went out of use altogether. Its place was
macy of abruptly taken by the socketed celt, which suddenly appears
socketed in great numbers in all the hoards belonging to Period VI.?
     celt Moulds for its manufacture were

Pilin, Tiszaféldvar, Fels6 Kubin, &c.t The socketed celt?
now becomes the characteristic implement in North Hungary
as in the Lausitz province. Sickles of the Italian and button
types are common. Razors were manufactured at Pilin,3 some Razors
conforming to the terremare type (Plate 5, Bz), others copying
the later Benacci II crescent.                      |
   The battle-axe, the favourite weapon of North Hungary
in the Middle Bronze Age, disappears almost as completely
as the shaft-hole working axe;‘ its place is taken by slashing
swords. The bronze hilted series begins with foreign types ap- Swords:
pearing already in Period VI: B3 from Forro in Abauj,> B4 from Foreign
Buzita in Abauj,° Aranyos in Borsod,’ the Danube at Pest® and types
Bga from Buzita, Piricse,? &c. From these foreign types deve-
loped local forms even before the end of Period VI. In B5 the Native
grip is encircled with ribs representing binding thongs, and develop-
there is a nick high up in the blade to prevent the sword ments
shaking out of the scabbard. The swelling blade seems in-
spired by a reminiscence of the old short sword illustrated at
Hajdu Samson. The new native Hungarian product was, as
we saw,” already being imported into the Tyrol, Switzerland,
 and Wurtemberg in phase E of the Late Bronze Age; there is
 an example even from Upper Italy." Another local type found
 in hoards with the foregoing, but possibly outliving it, has a
 saucer-shaped pommel. It, too, was exported widely, but
 especially to Galicia,’ where Bs also occurs frequently.
    Side by side with the foregoing and, as far as the closed finds Series C
 allow us to judge, first in company with the foreign types at
 Aranyos,% appear swords of series C. These must, therefore,
 rank in the first place as intruders; but they underwent a
 local development on the same lines as the bronze-hilted
 weapons. Finally, we may note a single antennae sword from
 Felka in county Spis (Szepes, Slovakia),’® and a rapier of type
 A3 from Rima-Szombat.”®

Spear-     After the sword the most important item in the warrior’s
 heads   equipment was the spear. These have now lanceolate or rhom-
         boid blades in contradistinction to the Middle Bronze Age
         leaf-shaped type."
   Mace     Finally, a distinctive North Hungarian weapon was the little
         mace of Pl. 15, 2 that may result from a lingering affection
         for the battle-axe. It is common in hoards from the beginning
         of Period VI, and was exported to Bohemia’ and Carniola.>
  Orna-     In addition to ornaments found in graves, we may mention
  ~ments in particular the coil shown in the middle row of Fig. 209.4 The
         type is commonest in North Hungary, where it appears already
         in the early hoard of Forrd,5 but it was exported to Western
         Hungary and the Banat. The same hoard and others show that
         cylinders were still being worn thoughout Period VI, but
         these were apparently longer and tighter than in Period V. In
         addition girdle plates of hammered bronze now appear. And
         among the pins a partiality for giant sizes ° is noticeable as in
    Gold contemporary deposits of Wurtemberg.          Among the beads,
   beads the gold specimens from Angyalféld7 found with the cup of Fig.
         208, deserve notice; they illustrate the same four-pointed star
         that we noticed in faience in Period V. But now the rays are
         wrinkled like the pottery warts, as in the similar glass beads
         from Croatian cemeteries of Period VII. The same hoard con- |
         tained a small pendant in the shape of a broad-tailed bird of
         bronze sheathed in gold leaf ornamented with stamped circles.
Fibulae     The development of big pins was, however, checked by
         the adoption of the fibula during Period VI. Though no
         brooches are found in tombs, North Hungary in the Late
         Bronze Age becomes definitely a fibula province.’ The local
         series begins with Ar found on the western edge of the pro-
         vince at Haidehof Puszta (Mosony). From this an indepen-
         dent local series develops parallel to the harp and spectacle
         types farther west and keeps the exaggerated spiral catch-
         plate throughout. But A3 was perhaps introduced from the
         head of the Adriatic,? since there are specimens also in West

        Hungary.'      In any case it took root in North Hungary, where

VI (e.g. at Rima-Szombat, Fig. 209) are generally interpreted
as horse-trappings. And from Zsujta (Abauj) comes a bird-
shaped top from a chariot-pole that can be little if any later.?
Another chariot part comes from the depot of Komjath that
belongs definitely to Period VII. It is therefore clear that the
people of North Hungary drove, even if they did not ride. They
must have controlled their steeds with bone-ended bits as in
Period V.
   Metal vessels are very common: we have both smooth? and Metal
bossed3 cups of hammered bronze (Fig. 210) and similar cups

shapes in gold (Fig. 208) that belong in part to Period VI.
Especially on the eastern frontier of the province many large

loops that are attached to T-pieces (Fig. 211) are represented and
in no less than five hoards just across the Tisza.4 Big buckets buckets
decorated with cireles flanked by birds’ heads in repoussé>
(Fig. 212) are found at three sites in the same area.
   All this beaten bronze ware is usually called Italian, but, as The
we have noted, doubts have been cast on the Italian origin of bronze

          the smooth cups and the T-cauldrons. Moreover, it would be
          manifestly absurd to contend that the gold cups from Angyal-
          féld (Fig. 208) were made in Italy. On the other hand, North
          Hungary would be a convenient centre for their diffusion. ‘Thus
          the T-cauldrons are found in Galicia, where undeniably North
          Hungarian swords are common, and in Transylvania* and West
          Hungary,? where fibulae of type As likewise occur. The
          smooth cups recur in the Ukraine.3
             It was evident that the North Hungarian culture was de-
Industry finitely based on the exploitation of the copper ores in which
      and the area it occupies is so rich. In the course of the foregoing
    trade analysis it will have become plain that North Hungary was also
          the home of a vigorous secondary industry. That is attested by
          the wealth and variety of original types as much as by the size
          of the workshops discovered at Pilin, Kisterenne, and Aszod.
          We can also detect evidence of industrial specialization. We
          find hoards composed exclusively of swords (Podhering,
          Zsujta) or of bronze vases (Hajdu Samson). Naturally, there-
          fore, not only metal but finished products of metallurgy were
          exported far and wide. We have seen that undeniably North
          Hungarian objects reached Carniola, North Italy, Switzerland,
          Wurtemberg, Galicia, and Moldavia.             If we accept the buckets
          as Hungarian we can add North Germany.’ The reflex of this
          trade is seen in the foreign objects that reached the industrial
          centres. Intercourse with the north brought amber to Géddllé5
          and Pilin.© The eastern connexions brought the Scythian
          objects that herald the collapse of the Bronze Age civilization.
    Italy From Italy came at least the fibulae of type A3, perhaps also Aq.
          Later we find at Aggtelek pottery adorned with little bronze
          knobs as at Sta. Lucia and Este.7’ And whether the buckets be
          Italian or no, the bird’s head motive’ adorning them is very com-
          mon in the Peninsula, and must either have reached Hungary
          from there or from a short-lived intermediate centre east of the
 No con- Adriatic.     On the other hand, direct relations with the Aegean
tact with have ceased.     The first Greek object to reach our area is the

                            AND    THE SCYTHS                                377
archaic hydria from Bene, dated to the sixth century and intro-
duced with the Scyths. Nevertheless the swords of type
Bs are adorned with an early Mycenaean pulley-motive ?
which is also seen on bone plaques from Tiszafiired 3 and bone
pommels from Tészeg D. Parallels from Vattina show that
this pattern came up the Danube, but it must already have be-
come naturalized in the Banat long before it was applied to
Late Bronze Age swords in North Hungary. Possibly too the
solid swans or ducks that appear perched on a mace flew up
the Danube. The earliest European resting-place of this
Asiatic bird was on the rim of a cup found in a submycenaean
hoard at Tiryns.t But probably he winged his way thence up
the Adriatic.
   If many of the foreign types found in North Hungary were Invasion
due to trade, that will not account for the abrupt appearance of need to
a whole series of new tools and weapons at the beginning of our explain
period. In particular the replacement of the neat shaft-hole new
axe by the socketed celt, a much inferior device, can only be forms
explained in ethnic terms. And the general adoption of the
sword instead of the battle-axe was scarcely less revolutionary.
These changes in armament, like the sudden introduction of
fluted decoration and a whole series of Lausitz forms in the
pottery, were the symbols of an actual conquest of North
Hungary by the Lausitz people. If old traditions survived, they
were preserved at first by a subject population. The North
Hungarian province of the Late Bronze Age belongs essentially
to the north rather than the south. Yet the older elements
asserted themselves more and more as time went on.
   The chronology of the period is fixed both stratigraphically Chrono-
and typologically. The intrusive fluted ware appears in ‘T’észeg logy
D, and the same level contains bone pommels adorned with the
same pulley-pattern as the swords of type B5. ‘The oldest
intrusive types that appear in bulk characterize the first half of
the Late Bronze Age—Reinecke D—while the earlier North
Hungarian exports (e.g. the mace from Repec) are found in
deposits of the same phase in Bohemia. The development of
the industry in the second phase is still better attested by finds
of North Hungarian swords in the Hallstatt A deposits of
Bavaria and Wurtemberg and by the strict parallelism between
